meta.page_title: How it was developing to our times?
meta.source_title: When Internet First appeared in Ukraine?

content.text: How it developed to our times
Next will do more detailed official explanation about all the years till now(2026), I will write short answer in italics near the year.
1990s : first internet in (post)Soviet space.

The first Internet users appeared in Ukraine during Soviet times. In 1990, the first three network access nodes were created (via Moscow to Finland) and support for the Ukrainian part of the Internet domain space — the ".ua" domain — began  . At first, it was supported informally, and on December 1, 1992, it was officially delegated to Ukraine. From that time on, the history of the Ukrainian segment of the Internet began .

By the end of 1991, the provider "Technosoft" served subscribers in all major cities of Ukraine. The speed was 2.5 kilobits per second. In February 1993, Uarnet, together with the Swedish Space Corporation, built the first satellite communication channel in Ukraine (via the Tele-X satellite ), which became the first direct connection in Ukraine to the global Internet .
In December 1999, Aiken Studio was created  - one of the first "smithies" of Ukrainian websites .
2000 IAU
In November 2000, the Internet Association of Ukraine (IAU) was established.
2006 the rise of the Internet in Ukraine
In January 2006, the size of the Ukrainian Internet audience was 4,207,391 unique users according to the website Sputnikmedia.net (out of reach) .

The website internetworldstats.com (out of reach) gives a figure of 5,278,100 users as of March 2005, which is 11.5% of the population of Ukraine. The increase in the Internet audience in Ukraine in 2000–2007 was 2539.1%. Since in 2000 there were 200,000 users and this was approximately 0.4% of the total population of Ukraine.
The website " Suspilne "  estimated the number of Internet users at over 4 million in 2006.
2007 the first torrent tracker

According to the press service of the Department of Communications and Informatization of the Ministry of Transport and Communications, as of January 1, 2007, there were about 9 million users in Ukraine. This is approximately 18.75% of the population of Ukraine.
According to TNS Ukraine, about 7 million users access the Internet at least once a week.
The first free Ukrainian torrent tracker  , Hurtom, has been launched .
2008 10 million people used internet
May
The number of Ukrainian Internet users in May 2008 increased by 0.77% compared to April and amounted to 8,471,954 unique users per month  .

In terms of geographical distribution, the Kyiv region held the lead . Its share was 58.82% of the total number of visitors. This was followed by Odessa , Dnipro , Donetsk , Kharkiv , Lviv , Zaporizhia , and Crimea . Their total share was 30.89%. The least activity of Internet users was noted in Chernivtsi and Zhytomyr regions  - only 0.17% each. Other regions accounted for 9.95% of users.
According to the then data of TNS Ukraine and the research company InMind , the number of users of the Ukrainian Internet was 10 million subscribers.
October

The size of the Ukrainian audience of Internet users (unique users who made more than one page view in October 2008 and users who viewed pages in October and September) amounted to 10,164,517 people as of the end of October, which is 6% more than in
September  .
In percentage terms: Kyiv was ahead by a significant margin — 58.96% of all Internet users in Ukraine.

Next were Odesa (6.43%), Dnipro (5.62%), Donetsk (5.41%), Kharkiv (4.44%), Lviv (3.58%), Zaporizhia (2.47%), Crimea (2.70%). The total share of these regions in percentage terms is 30.65%. Other regions of Ukraine accounted for less than 10% of Internet users.
The lowest rates are: Lutsk (0.17%), Zhytomyr (0.21%), and Chernivtsi (0.22%).
December

41% of Ukrainians living in cities with a population of over 50,000 used the Internet . The number of Internet users in cities was 6.638 million, with the largest age group being 30-39-year-old Ukrainians (22.8%). The typically large “youth” group (14-19 years old) used network services somewhat less — 19.3%. There were even fewer Internet users aged 40-49 — 15%. Overall, 43.2% of the population in the 25-40 age group used the Internet.
87% of the Internet audience visited the World Wide Web at least once a week, while 44% visited once or more times a day.
The incomes of 18% of Internet users were considered high and 66% were considered average.
2009  30% of Ukraine used internet daily.
March

The Ukrainian audience of Internet users (users who made more than one page view in March 2009, and users who viewed pages in March and in the previous month) in March 2009 amounted to 11.96 million people, which is 9.4% more than in February 2009.

In the regional distribution of users, Kyiv was the leader by a significant margin, accounting for 60.14% of all Internet users in Ukraine in March. It was followed by Odesa (5.14%), Kharkiv (5.39%), Dnipro (4.92%), Donetsk (4.82%), Lviv (2.83%), Crimea (2.80%), Zaporizhia (1.80%). The total share of these regions was 24.9%.
The remaining regions of Ukraine accounted for 13.26% of users.
Outsiders in terms of Internet penetration in March 2009 were Lutsk (0.18%), Zhytomyr (0.21%), Chernivtsi (0.32%), and Transcarpathia (0.31%)  .
November

According to the results of the fall of 2009, the Internet audience in Ukraine amounted to 8.25 million unique users, which is 1.613 million more than the similar indicator in the fall of 2008. This was evidenced by the results of a study conducted by GfK Ukraine on behalf of the Ukrainian Association of Internet Advertising (UAIA).

The highest growth rate of Internet visitors was observed in cities with a population of 50-100 thousand inhabitants - the growth for the year was 14%. Also in 2009, the Internet was used much more actively - daily coverage in that year was 31% of Ukrainian residents in cities with a population of 50 thousand or more (including million-plus cities) compared to 18% in 2008 .
2010 12,900,000 Internet users in Ukraine

In April 2010, a study by Gemius Ukraine showed 8.669 million regular users aged 16 and older (i.e. an annual growth of 1.438 million compared to April 2009). At the same time, InMind data was published at the request of InAU : 12 million or Internet access coverage at 32%.
In June, Bigmir had 18,581,501 unique users  .
As of October 6, 2010, there were 12,900,000 Internet users in Ukraine, according to InMind .

33% of all residents of Ukraine visited the Internet at least once a month. Over eight million Internet users lived in large cities and towns with a population of more than 50 thousand, 4.6 million users lived in smaller settlements.
Of all Ukrainian Internet users aged 15 and over, 8.7 million used the Internet every day or almost every day.
The highest level of Internet penetration was among young age groups: 61% of the population aged 15 to 29 years used the world wide web, 39% of people aged 30 to 44 years  .
2011 90% of Kyiv had access to internet

According to the results of a study conducted by the International Center for Policy Studies ( ICPS ), published in September 2011 in the WEEKLYUA magazine, a comparative situation with access to the Internet by residents of different regions of Ukraine was published . The least opportunities for access were for residents of the Rivne region, where the figure barely reached 2%, which in international terms corresponded to the level of Afghanistan . The best opportunities for choosing Internet access services were for residents of Kyiv, where about 90% of Kyiv residents were users of the World Wide Web, which in international terms corresponded to the level of the Netherlands .

“In some metropolitan high-rise buildings, 14 cable operators are ready to provide their services to residents at once, while in many small towns and villages, people are still forced to occupy a telephone line to access the network.”— International Center for Advanced Studies, WEEKLYUA magazine
2012 48% of Ukrainians had access to internet
According to the analysis of the Internet audience in Ukraine , in March 2012, 48% of Ukrainian residents aged 15 and older had access to the Internet. The following people used the Internet:
42% (16.9 million) once a month or more
39% (15.7 million) once a week or more
31% (12.4 million) daily, or almost daily.

The number of Internet subscribers in Ukraine in the second quarter of 2012 compared to the second quarter of 2011 increased by 30% and amounted to 4.64 million people , moreover, more than 4.2 million of them were home subscribers.
2013 50% of Ukraine used and had internet

According to the Kyiv International Institute of Sociology (KIIS) , in September 2013, 49.8% of the adult population of Ukraine used the Internet. Thus, the growth in the number of users continued at an even faster pace than expected. The growth rate during February 2012 - October 2013 was 16%, which is slightly lower than the largest jump of 34% in the period from March 2011 to February 2012  .
Diagram of changes in the number of Internet users in Ukraine 2000-2013 (source: ITU)
A less optimistic figure of 41.8% was published  by the International Telecommunication Union .
2014 More Ukrainian bloggers

In recent years, especially after 2014, a large number of Ukrainian-language YouTube channels for children have appeared  . The number of Ukrainian-language bloggers on YouTube has been steadily increasing .
2015 “Telecom Ukraine 2015”- Ukrainian conference

In September 2015, the largest event of the year for the telecom industry took place in the city of Odessa - the All-Ukrainian Conference "Telecom Ukraine 2015", which brought together over 300 participants - heads of enterprises in the communications, informatization and telecommunications sectors, state representatives, public figures, world manufacturers of modern telecommunications equipment and other delegates interested in the development of the domestic industry.

The initiators and organizers of the Conference were InAU, State Enterprise "UA-IX", Telecom Summit and Telecom Fiesta. Having joined forces to prepare such a popular event, the organizers set themselves the goal of uniting the largest possible number of participants not only from the telecommunications industry of Ukraine, but also representatives of cable TV and the media industry, in order to involve all components of the domestic informatization industry in communication and interaction with each other and with the state. The following specialized associations were also invited to cooperate and took an active part in the preparation of the program: APITU , APPK, TelPU, VAOKKT and Ukrtelemerezha  .

The number of Internet users in 2015 continued to grow faster than expected. The growth rate during February 2015–February 2016 was 8%. Longer-term comparisons have not been possible since 2014, as the situation in the country changed dramatically due to the aggression by the Russian Federation . Nevertheless, despite all the difficulties, Internet use has been steadily growing—in the period from February 2014 to February 2016 alone, the number of Internet users increased by 7 percentage points, and the growth rate was 13%  .
2016 91% of teenagers used internet

According to data from the KIIS (published on their official website on May 4, 2016), which presented the results of a survey conducted during February 2016, at the beginning of the year 61.6% of the adult population of Ukraine used the Internet. The share of Internet users among people aged 18-39 in Ukraine reached 91%. Older age and living in rural areas significantly reduced the likelihood of using the Internet. The survey was conducted in all regions of Ukraine except for the occupied Crimea and the temporarily uncontrolled territories of Donbass  .
The National Bank's 2015 Annual Report was first distributed exclusively through online channels in two versions — a full-color version for viewing and an ECO version for printing  .
2017 more than 60% of adults used internet

According to the Internet Association of Ukraine (distributed on their official website in April 2017), which presented the results of a survey conducted during February 2017, at the beginning of the year 64.7% of the adult population of Ukraine used the Internet. The share of Internet visitors among people aged 15-29 in Ukraine reached 97%. Older age and living in rural areas significantly reduced the likelihood of using the Internet. The survey was conducted in all regions of Ukraine except for the occupied Crimea and the temporarily uncontrolled territories of Donbas. The number of Internet users continued to grow. Most often, they accessed the Internet using a stationary PC  - 51%, mobile phones  - 50%, laptops  - 42%, tablet computers  - 21%, stationary PCs at work - 8%, laptops at work - 5%. About 21.6 million users used the Internet in Ukraine. Women made up 51% of all users, men 49%. Villagers made up 27% of the total, while among all village residents, 53% of them used the Internet  .
2018 UAH 62 billion(in Ukraine currency) to communications market

As of the end of 2018, thousands of business entities were operating in the telecommunications market, most of them being individual entrepreneurs. The main indicators of the industry for 2018 were as follows: the total annual revenue of the communications market amounted to UAH(in Ukraine currency) 62 billion, which is 10.2% more compared to 2017. Almost half of this - UAH 35 billion - was the revenue of mobile operators. The growth rate for the reporting year 2018 was 11% compared to 2017. In 2018, mobile operators invested a total of about UAH 10 billion in the industry (excluding the cost of 4G licenses)  . The most visited sites at the end of the year were: google, youtube.com, facebook.com, ukr.net, vkontakte (vk.com), olx.ua, privatbank.ua, yandex, odnoklassniki (ok.ru), sinoptik.ua, instagram.com, prom.ua.
2019 85% of teenagers used internet daily
According to Gemius, as of June 2019, there were 24.8 million Internet users in Ukraine.
According to the annual Kantar Ukraine survey (out of reach now) 2019, 74% of the Ukrainian population (aged 12-70) used the Internet. 85 percent of them used it every day.
19 million Ukrainians used their own smartphones to access the Internet.
In 2019, Ukraine ranked only 82nd, despite the fact that in 2018, it was in 58th place in the ranking of countries with high-speed Internet .

At the same time, as of September 2019, Ukraine ranked fourth among all EU -39 countries in terms of the number of FTTH/B-nodes (Fiber To The Building) and FTTH (Fiber To The Home) — 11.24 million units. Only Spain, France, and Russia had more. In total, there were about 400 million FTTH/B-nodes in the EU-39, half of which were in the TOP-5 countries .

Despite the fact that in 2019 Ukrainians began to trust the media much less (trust in all traditional media fell by 11% compared to 2018), and trust in particular fluctuates between 19% in the national press, 22% in local radio, and 49% in national TV channels — according to USAID-Internews, 51% of respondents trusted national online media .
2020 from 19 million to 26 million because of coronavirus

During the year of quarantine related to the COVID-19 coronavirus pandemic, from the beginning of 2020 to the beginning of 2021, that is, in 2020, the Ukrainian social media audience increased by 7 million people, according to a report by GlobalLogic , citing the results of a study conducted based on open data.

“At the beginning of 2020, there were 19 million [social media] users in Ukraine, in 2021 the figure reached 26 million. At the same time, the penetration of social media has increased by half: currently 60% of the country's population is registered with them, while in January 2020 it was just over 40%,” this message notes .

In general, digital culture is actively developing in Ukraine, because from October 2020 to January 2021 alone, the number of 5G smartphones in Ukraine increased from 31 thousand to 194 thousand units  . At the same time, the number of 4G smartphones has increased from 30 million to 39.9 million units during the same time.
2021 30 millions users
As of early 2021, the number of Ukrainian Internet users was almost 30 million, or about 67% of the country's population  .

In 2021, the Internet overtook television as a source of information in Ukraine for the first time. According to a survey conducted by Research&Branding Group, 51% of Ukrainians surveyed prefer the Internet as a source of information, while 44% prefer television, and 2% prefer radio and print media.

Also, in 2021, Ukrainians began to encounter scammers on the Internet twice as often. Cybercriminals most often attack users through popular instant messengers, and email is almost not in demand during online shopping.

Over the past six months, 45% of Ukrainians surveyed have encountered online fraud attempts. Last year, this figure was half as low — 22%. Most often, since the beginning of 2021, residents of large cities have been attacked — ⅔ of cases . Also, according to Verizon, in 2021, 36% of cybersecurity breaches were related to phishing attacks, during which attackers disguised themselves as employees of institutions that inspire trust among users and demand confidential information. The main topic used by scammers in 2021 was the coronavirus pandemic . Attackers changed their phishing campaigns depending on the news.
2022 62th place in Ookla speed test

Ukraine has risen 15 places in the global mobile internet speed ranking, according to the Ookla Speedtest Global Index, from 77th place in January 2021 to 62nd place in January 2022. According to Ookla research, the average download speed on the mobile network increased by almost 10 Mbps — from 19.66 Mbps at the beginning of 2021 to 29.06 Mbps in 2022 .
2023 - 2024 speed of 80.2 Mbps

According to the rating of the popular resource Speedtest , which measures the speed of online connections, it became known that in 2024 Ukraine occupies one of the last places in the world in terms of mobile Internet performance. According to data for June 2024, the country occupies 93rd place out of 108 measured, with an average speed of 21.24 Mbps. This is 2.5 times lower than the world average, which is marked by the insufficient efficiency of the Internet infrastructure on mobile networks in Ukraine. The situation with fixed Internet in Ukraine is somewhat better: Ukraine occupies 70th place among 159 countries with a speed of 80.2 Mbps .
So for summary till 2020 it was rising after that it started to encounter a lot of problems, don’t get me started to talk about them(war)
But it’s all wonderfull but let’s look on more talking statictic of how many people used internet in Ukraine:
Data from database: World Development Indicators Last Updated: 10/14/2016 indicates the data below.
Differencies from other countries and a little bit more about money
By mid-2012, Ukraine was among the top ten countries in Europe in terms of the number of Internet users   — up to 15 million Ukrainians had access to the World Wide Web.

In Ukraine, about a third (33%) of the population were Internet users  , and, for example, according to internetworldstats.com  , in the USA 69.6% of the population uses the Internet, in Germany 61.3%, in the UK 50.3%, in France 50.3%, in Poland (62%) and in Russia (44%). In 2012, 50.9% of the population of the European Union used the World Wide Web.
In terms of the cost of home ( fixed ) Internet, Ukraine ranked " third from the bottom "  among 160 countries according to the International Telecommunication Union rating .
In 2012, the average monthly cost of Internet access in Ukraine was just over 75 hryvnias .

As of the end of 2020, compared to other countries, Internet access in Ukraine was quite inexpensive. The cheapest package cost $ 3.6 per month, and the most expensive — $13.5. The average cost of network access was $6.5 (about 180 UAH). For comparison, a package in the PRC and Georgia cost a little more than $12 per month. In Lithuania, the cheapest package cost $3.5 per month (cheaper than in Ukraine), the most expensive — $28.2. The average monthly cost is $12.9. On average, Internet access in France and Italy cost $31.7 per month. In the UK, such a service is even more expensive. The cheapest package cost $23, the most expensive — $68. The average cost per month was $35.7  .
